findings.title: Privileged container detected
findings.why_it_matters: A privileged container has full access to host devices and can trivially escape to the host.
findings.remediation: Remove --privileged flag. Grant only the specific capabilities needed with --cap-add.

findings.title: Docker socket exposed
findings.why_it_matters: Access to the Docker socket allows full control over the container runtime, enabling container escape and host compromise.
findings.remediation: Remove the socket mount. If API access is needed, use a read-only proxy like docker-socket-proxy.

findings.title: Seccomp disabled
findings.why_it_matters: Without seccomp filtering, the container can invoke any syscall, greatly increasing the kernel attack surface.
findings.remediation: Use the default Docker seccomp profile or apply a custom profile with --security-opt seccomp=<profile>.

findings.title: Sensitive environment variables detected (3)
findings.why_it_matters: Secrets in environment variables are visible to any process in the container and may leak through logs or debug endpoints.
findings.remediation: Use mounted secrets files or a secrets manager instead of environment variables for sensitive data.

findings.title: no_new_privs not set
findings.why_it_matters: Without no_new_privs, processes can gain additional privileges through setuid binaries or capability-aware programs.

findings.title: Kubernetes API server reachable
findings.why_it_matters: API server reachability combined with a service account token means the pod can interact with the Kubernetes API.
findings.remediation: Use NetworkPolicy to restrict API server access from pods that don't need it.
